cases.title: Tactical Casualty Behind Cover
cases.presentation: A casualty wearing body armor was shot in the left chest. He has severe respiratory distress, cyanosis, and you cannot palpate a radial pulse. Breath sounds are absent on the left with hyperresonance. You are still in a tactical environment.
cases.question: What is the best approach to decompression here?
options: Remove the body armor to use the anterior site
Use the lateral approach (4th–5th ICS anterior axillary line) without removing armor
Apply a chest seal and wait
cases.explanation: This is a tension pneumothorax with shock and respiratory distress—decompression is indicated. In a tactical setting the lateral approach can be performed without removing body armor and may more reliably reach the pleural space.
cases.clinicalPearl: Know both approaches: the lateral site is invaluable when armor or positioning makes the anterior site impractical.

cases.title: Failed First Attempt — Catheter Too Short
cases.presentation: You decompress a muscular 110-kg patient anteriorly with a short 3-cm catheter, but there is no air rush and no improvement. He remains in extremis with absent left breath sounds and JVD.
cases.question: What is the most likely problem and the fix?
options: The diagnosis is wrong — stop decompression
The catheter was too short to reach the pleural space — repeat with a 6–9 cm catheter
cases.explanation: A short catheter frequently fails to reach the pleural space in muscular or obese patients. Repeat the decompression with an adequately long large-bore catheter (6–9 cm), or use the lateral approach.
cases.clinicalPearl: Match catheter length to chest-wall thickness—the most common reason for a 'failed' decompression is a catheter that never reached the pleura.

cases.title: Re-accumulation En Route
cases.presentation: A blunt-trauma patient improved dramatically after needle decompression, with a returning radial pulse. Twenty minutes later en route he again becomes hypoxic and hypotensive with rising JVD and absent breath sounds on the same side.
cases.question: What is the appropriate action?
options: Assume the original device fixed it permanently
Reassess and repeat decompression for a re-accumulating tension pneumothorax
Remove the original catheter and do nothing
cases.explanation: The catheter can kink, clot, or dislodge, allowing the tension pneumothorax to re-accumulate. Recurrent signs warrant reassessment and repeat decompression, with continued transport for definitive chest tube placement.
cases.clinicalPearl: Decompression is temporary—reassess frequently and be ready to decompress again if tension returns.
